coredata.dc:title: Hydrophobic self-cleaning micro-nano composite polyethylene-based agricultural plastic film with light conversion and abrasion resistance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Chu, Xiaohong
Zhang, Pan
Shi, Shaoze
Liu, Yihan
Feng, Wenli
Zhou, Ninglin
Li, Jun
Shen, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Agricultural plastic film (APF) is extensively used in modern agriculture. However, to the best of our knowledge, the self-cleaning property and light transmittance of APF are still the main factors affecting crop yield at present. To solve these problems, for the first time, we developed polyethylene-based dust-proof composite APF with multi-scale micro-nano structure and light conversion performance. By combining the carbon dots (CDs) and hydrophobic silica nanoparticles (FSi-SiO2) with organically modified montmorillonite (OMMT), composite materials with multi-scale micro-nano structure and low surface energy (SFE) were constructed (FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT). After blending with linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE, PE), a self-cleaning dust-proof APF with “lotus-effect” was synthesized (PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT). The PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibited excellent hydrophobic property, which had a water contact angle (WCA) of 108.9°. The results of the mechanical properties tests showed that the horizontal and portrait tensile strength of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT improved from 25.08 and 27.41 MPa to 33.14 and 42.21 MPa and that the horizontal and portrait elongation at break improved from 273.53 % and 380.23–369.43 % and 480.73 %, respectively. In addition, the anti-fouling and dust-proof performance were studied, and the results showed that the anti-fouling rate and dust removal efficiency of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were reached to 99.84 % and 99.58 %, respectively. Additionally, PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT also possessed excellent thermal stability, anti-algae and anti-bacterial adhesion properties, and light conversion performance. This work could provide a facile approach and strategy to fabricate functional APF for agricultural application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Agricultural plastic film
Self-cleaning
Light conversion
Lotus-effect
Hydrophobic property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT
Surface wettability and abrasion resistance PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT
Anti-fouling and dust-proof performance of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT
Anti-algae and anti-bacterial adhesion performance of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was supported by Jiangsu Science and Technology Plan ( BE 2019391 ), Key agricultural R & D program of Jiangsu Province (Modern Agriculture) ( BE 2020347 ), Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, Shandong Natural Science Foundation ( ZR2021LSW001 , ZR2021MC017 , ZR2020KH019 , ZR201807060862 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) MMT, (b) OMMT, (c) FSi-SiO2@MMT, and (d) FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT, respectively, insets are SEM images of corresponding samples.

(a–c) SEM images of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. (d–f) Cross-sectional SEM images of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. (g-h) 3D CLSM images of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT.

(a) ATR-FTIR spectra of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. (b) XRD diffraction patterns of FSi-SiO2@MMT, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. (c) TGA curves of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. (d) UV transmittance curves of PE and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. (e) Excitation spectra of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT at Em = 426 nm. (f) PL spectra of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) The WCA and SFE of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. (b) The haze and transmittance curves of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. WCA of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT after (c) half-folding and (d) sandpaper abrasion assay, respectively.

(a) Percentage of retention of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. (b) The images of pollution retention on surface of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. (c) Dust removing efficiency of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT.
(a) Images of Chlorella growth and (b) Photos of bacterial colonies of E. coli and S. aureus in surface of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT.

head.ce:title: Hydrophobic self-cleaning micro-nano composite polyethylene-based agricultural plastic film with light conversion and abrasion resistance

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Agricultural plastic film (APF) is extensively used in modern agriculture. However, to the best of our knowledge, the self-cleaning property and light transmittance of APF are still the main factors affecting crop yield at present. To solve these problems, for the first time, we developed polyethylene-based dust-proof composite APF with multi-scale micro-nano structure and light conversion performance. By combining the carbon dots (CDs) and hydrophobic silica nanoparticles (FSi-SiO2) with organically modified montmorillonite (OMMT), composite materials with multi-scale micro-nano structure and low surface energy (SFE) were constructed (FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT). After blending with linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE, PE), a self-cleaning dust-proof APF with “lotus-effect” was synthesized (PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT). The PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibited excellent hydrophobic property, which had a water contact angle (WCA) of 108.9°. The results of the mechanical properties tests showed that the horizontal and portrait tensile strength of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT improved from 25.08 and 27.41 MPa to 33.14 and 42.21 MPa and that the horizontal and portrait elongation at break improved from 273.53 % and 380.23–369.43 % and 480.73 %, respectively. In addition, the anti-fouling and dust-proof performance were studied, and the results showed that the anti-fouling rate and dust removal efficiency of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were reached to 99.84 % and 99.58 %, respectively. Additionally, PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT also possessed excellent thermal stability, anti-algae and anti-bacterial adhesion properties, and light conversion performance. This work could provide a facile approach and strategy to fabricate functional APF for agricultural application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of modern agriculture, agricultural plastic film (APF) was widely used worldwide due to its performance of heat resistance, cold resistance, and moisture retention [1,2]. To meet the needs of crop growth, functional APF has attracted extensive attention. However, the outer surface of the functional APF was easily absorb number of electrostatic dust in the actual application process, which was attributed to the migration of various small-molecule additives such as dripping agents, anti-fogging agents, and antistatic agents, as well as the influence of light and thermal aging [3]. The light transmittance of the APF was reduced and the photosynthesis of plants was affected, thereby reducing the quality and yield of crops even increasing the economic burden of agricultural producers [4–6]. Therefore, developing a multifunctional agricultural plastic film, which possesses dust-proof and anti-fouling properties, is urgently needed. In recent years, the superhydrophobic materials have attracted extensive attention, which possess a water contact angle (WCA) above 150° and a sliding angle (SA) below 10°, due to their low surface energy (SFE) and high roughness [7–10]. They have number of advantages, including self-cleaning, anti-fouling, and dust-proof performance. Currently, most of superhydrophobic materials were applied in the dust-proof and self-cleaning of the surface of glass [11,12], electronic components [13–15], solar panels [16–18], and other substrates. Therefore, to solve the problem of low light transmittance of the APF, it is of great significance to design and develop a new superhydrophobic material as a dust-proof additives.

Montmorillonite (MMT) is an inexpensive and readily available natural clay mineral, which is a layered hydro-aluminosilicate mineral containing alkali and alkaline metal elements [19,20]. MMT is composed of two layers of silicon tetrahedra and one layer of aluminum octahedra and is a typical 2:1 layered clay mineral, exhibiting high surface areas [21]. There exist water and some exchange cations between the layers of the crystal structure, thus, the MMT possesses high cationic exchange capability, water absorption and retention property, and swelling capability, realizing broad applications in various fields [22–24]. Additionally, silica (SiO2) nanomaterials have attracted extensive attention owing to their excellent biocompatibility, super thermal stability, and easy surface modification [21,25]. Meera et al. developed SiO2 modified MMT clay composites for hydrophobic surface coatings through sol-gel polymerization technique using MMT clay and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) precursor [26]. Then the developed hybrids were coated on glass substrate, and the hydrophobic property was measured by WCA measurements. The obtained results indicated that the hydrophobic property of the coated surface was gradually enhanced with the increasing content of MMT, suggesting these hybrid coatings possess great potential as hydrophobic coatings to apply in leather substrate. Subasri et al. successfully developed superhydrophobic surfaces through adding nano-clay into hydrophobic SiO2 matrix under sol-gel process [27]. The obtained coating with nano-clay exhibits low SFE and dual-scale roughness, thus exhibiting super-hydrophobicity. The WCA and SA were measured to be 170° and < 10°, respectively, indicating the nano-clay exhibits great potential for preparation superhydrophobic coatings. Therefore, MMT and SiO2 could be regarded as ideal raw materials for the preparation of superhydrophobic composites.

An ideal superhydrophobic material surface should exhibit higher roughness and lower SA, which like lotus with multiple nano- and micro-scale ultra-structures [28–31]. Therefore, it is necessary to design a micro/nano multiscale material which possesses layered rough structure of suitable scale and fine microscopic gaps. Based on Cassie-Baxter surface construction technology [32–34], the micro/nano multiscale surface could form a larger-scale air cushion layer and the solid-liquid contact area was reduced, realizing the low adhesion of water droplets. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of carbon materials with abundant surface functional groups, good biocompatibility, unique photoluminescent performance, and ultrasmall size [35], which have been widely studied and applied in various fields including bioimaging [36,37], drug delivery [38,39], ion detection [40,41], catalysis [42,43], and so on. However, the use of CDs to construct superhydrophobic materials with multiscale roughness is rarely reported.

Based on the above consideration, for the first time, we reported polyethylene-based dust-proof composite APF which possesses blue fluorescence effect and multi-scale micro-nano structure. To improve the compatibility between MMT-based composites and linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE, PE), the silane coupling agents (3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl methacrylate, KH-570) and long-chain quaternary ammonium salts (Stearyltrimethylammonium Bromide, STAB) were chosen as modifier on the basis of previous work. The grafting of 1H, 1H, 2H, 2H-perfluoroalkyltriethoxysilanes (PFOTES) on the surface of organically modified montmorillonite (OMMT) could effectively decrease the SFE and enhance the hydrophobic performance of the polyethylene-based dust-proof composite APF. Additionally, the addition of CDs not only enhance the roughness of the composite but also could convert ultraviolet light, which is not conducive to plant photosynthesis, into blue light to improve the photosynthetic efficiency of plants. As illustrated in Scheme 1, a self-cleaning dust-proof APF with “lotus-effect” was successfully prepared (PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT). The hydrophobicity, surface energy, light transmittance, haze, thermal stability, and mechanical properties of the PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were investigated. Additionally, self-cleaning, dust-proof, anti-algal and anti-bacterial adhesion properties of the PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were also evaluated. Together, this research opened an avenue to APF with self-cleaning, dust-proof, anti-fouling, and light conversion performance. Thus, PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT possess great potential in agriculture.

FSi-SiO2@MMT were prepared according to the previously described procedure [44,45]. In brief, 3.4 g of TEOS, 7.1 g isopropyl alcohol (IPA), 0.27 g KH-570, and 5.56 g MMT were added into a three-necked flask and heated into 60 °C. The pH of the above mixture was adjusted to 4–5 using HCl (36–38 %), then stirred for 15 min 0.65 g of H2O was added dropwise to the above mixture, then 2.5 g PFOTES and 0.65 g H2O were added at the same rate and refluxed for 13 h under mechanical stirring at 500 rpm/min. Ultimately, the FSi-SiO2@MMT were obtained after drying and grinding. The synthesis process of FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was the same as FSi-SiO2@MMT. In the preparation process, the MMT and H2O were replaced by 5.56 g OMMT and 1.25 mL of CDs solution (0.02224 g/mL), respectively. Ultimately, the FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were obtained after drying and grinding.

PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were mixed with LLDPE in a ratio of 3:97, respectively, and added a few drops of glycerin. After stirring until the surface of the LLDPE pellets adhered to the sample powder, the mixture was added to the funnel of the double-layer coextrusion laboratory blown film machine. Then it was conveyed, extruded, and plasticized into a uniform melt by a screw, and extruded from the top of the extruder through twin-screw temperature control, quantity control and pressure control under a certain plasticizing pressure. At the exit position of the extruder, the melt was formed into a cylindrical shape, and the melt was put into the herringbone clamping part of the frame by stretching, and the clamped membrane body formed a closed cylindrical space. Then squeeze compressed air of constant pressure into the cylindrical space, and the membrane embryo formed a membrane bubble due to the expansion of the air. With the rotation and continuous pulling of the traction roller, the film bubble entered the guide roller and the winding device, and was continuously wound with constant tension to form a film roll to prepare PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT dust-proof composite films.

The abrasion resistance performance of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was evaluated by half-folding and sandpaper abrasion experiments [46]. Firstly, the PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT composite films were cut into 2.5 cm × 5 cm. For half-folding assay: The cut PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT composite films were subjected to forward folding and reverse folding experiments of 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 times, respectively, and the WCA of the surface under the corresponding folding times was measured simultaneously. For sandpaper abrasion assay: The cut PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT composite films were placed on 1000-mesh gauze paper, respectively, and a mass of 50 g was placed on the surface of the composite films. Then, the two composite films were moved to the left front at a constant speed, and the WCA of the worn composite films was recorded every 10 cm of movement.

The anti-fouling and dust-proof performance of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT composite films were evaluated by self-cleaning assay [47], dust-proof assay, anti-algae adhesion assay [48], and anti-bacterial adhesion assay [49].

To investigate the self-cleaning performance of these films, 0.3 g soil was added to 50 mL methylene blue solution (1 mg/mL) to form blue thin mud (BTM) solution. These composite films were cut into 2.5 cm × 5 cm and fixed on glass slides. Then, these glass slides were placed obliquely (45°), and 2 mL of BTM solution was dropped on the surface of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT, respectively. After that, the BTM solution adhering to these composite films needs to be photographed and recorded. The mass of residual BTM (mrBTM) solution was calculated by the following formula:
mrBTM=mx-m0
where mx represents the mass of residual BTM solution, PE-based composite films, and glass slides; m0 represents the mass of PE-based composite films and glass slides.

In brief, PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT composite films were fixed on a flat cardboard sheet and weighed. Then, about 1.02 g of dust was placed on the surface of the PE-based composite films. By simulating the natural wind environment, blow air with a fan at a distance of 20 cm from the PE-based composite films. The flat cardboard sheet with PE-based composite films fixed were weighed at 5 min, 10 min, 20 min, 40 min and 60 min, respectively. The dust removing efficiency of PE-based composite films at different times were calculated by the following formula:
Dust removing efficiency ( %)=(m0-mt)/m0×100 %
where m0 represents the initial mass of dust and flat cardboard sheet with PE-based composite films fixed; mt represents the mass of dust and flat cardboard sheet with PE-based composite films fixed at time t.

Briefly, PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT composite films were cut into 2.5 × 2.5 cm and washed with H2O and EtOH for several times, then, dried in an oven. After 10 mL of Chlorella was added into petri dish, PE-based composite films were immersed in the above Chlorella fluid, respectively. At 1, 3, 5 and 7 days, the growth and adhesion of Chlorella on the surface of the PE-based composite films were photographed and recorded by a microscope.

The anti-bacterial adhesion performance of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT composite films was evaluated by plate counting method. Firstly, 30 µL of the bacterial suspension was added into to a sterile centrifuge tube containing 3 mL of liquid Luria-Bertani (LB) culture medium. After the PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT composite films were cut into 1 × 1 cm, these PE-based composite films were immersed in the above bacterial solution, respectively, and then co-cultured for 24 h. After that, the PE-based composite films were taken out and placed in a sterile centrifuge tube containing 3 mL of LB culture medium, sonicated for 2 min with an ultrasonic cleaner. Afterwards, these bacterial solutions were diluted to 105, 107, and 109 times, respectively, then, taking 30 µL to spread on the agar plates and incubated for 12 h at 37 °C. The colonies on the agar plate were then photographed and recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT
ce:section.ce:para: The FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were synthesized by in situ hydrolysis and co-polycondensation reaction, then melt blend intercalation with LLDPE pellets, respectively, to obtain PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT composite films (Scheme 1). The morphologies of MMT, FSi-SiO2@MMT, OMMT, and FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were observed by SEM and TEM ( Fig. 1). As displayed in Fig. 1a-b, the TEM and SEM images of MMT and OMMT exhibit a distinct sheet-like structure with a size of about 1 µm. Compared with MMT, the interlayer spacing of OMMT was expanded, and the lamellae were more dispersed. The TEM and SEM images of FSi-SiO2@MMT displayed that SiO2 with a size of about 50 nm was successfully loaded onto the MMT surface (Fig. 1c). Fig. 1d and Figs. S1–2 revealed that SiO2 (white circles) and CDs (red circles) with a size of about 30 nm and 5 nm, respectively, were successfully loaded on the surface of OMMT, and the CDs were distributed in the voids of spherical SiO2. Additionally, the HRTEM images of FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT displayed that SiO2 and CDs possessed good crystallinity and formed continuous crystal phase with distinct lattice stripe spacing of 0.38 nm and 0.21 nm, respectively (Figs. S1–2). It is worth noting that SiO2, CDs, and OMMT constitute multi-scale micro-nano structures of composite superhydrophobic materials. Compared with FSi-SiO2@MMT, FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT possess the following advantages: 1) The amount of SiO2 supported on the surface of OMMT increases and was uniformly and tightly dispersed; 2) OMMT sheets were not packed together and the active hydroxyl functional groups on their surface were fully utilized; 3) The addition of CDs enabled the FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT to possess multi-dimensional roughness, and successfully constructed a composite superhydrophobic material with a biomimetic “lotus leaf” structure. The morphology and roughness of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were observed by SEM and 3D CLSM, as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. S3. The surface of PE film was smooth and the surface of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT films were rough (Fig. 2a-c). The increase in surface roughness of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT was mainly attributed to the micro-scale lamellar structure of MMT itself and the nanoscale SiO2 grafted on the surface of MMT. Compared with PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, the increase in surface roughness of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was more obvious owing to the presence of multiscale micro-nano structures, including SiO2, CDs, and OMMT. As shown in Fig. 2d, the cross-sectional SEM image of PE was smooth and flat. Due to the different morphologies of FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT, the profile roughness of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was higher than that of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT (Fig. 2e-f). The nanoscale structure of the fracture surface of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT mainly contains SiO2 with a size of about 50 nm, which was relatively dispersed on the surface of the MMT sheet, forming loose micro-nano composite micro-gap. However, the nanoscale structure of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT fracture surface mainly contained SiO2 and CDs, which were densely distributed on the micron-scale OMMT sheet, thus the SiO2, CDs, and OMMT together formed a dense micro-nano composite micro-gap. The cross-sectional SEM images of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibited the same results of SEM. The mapping and EDS analysis results showed that both PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT contain C, Si, O, Al, and F elements, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT also contains N element, suggesting PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were successfully prepared (Fig. S4-S5). The surface roughness and microstructure of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT composite films were further confirmed by 3D CLSM. The higher the number of peak protrusions and the more obvious the change in peak height, the rougher the surface of the material. As shown in Fig. 2g-h and Fig. S3, compared with PE and PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, the 3D CLSM image of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibited more peak number and height variation, indicating the roughness of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was more obvious.

Attenuated total internal reflectance fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) spectra of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were carried out to identify their chemical composition and structure. As shown in Fig. 3a, PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were all found to exhibit obvious characteristic peaks at 2917, 2848, 1461, and 721 cm−1. The peaks at 2917 and 2848 cm−1 were attributed to the -CH2 symmetrical and antisymmetric stretching vibration of PE, the peak at 1461 cm−1 was ascribed to the -CH2 deformation bending vibration, and the peak at 721 cm−1 was assigned to the -CH2 in-plane rocking vibration. Compared with PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibit new absorption bands at 1022 cm−1 due to Si-O-Si symmetric stretching vibration, indicating FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were successfully incorporated into the LLDPE matrix. Fig. 3b displayed the XRD patterns of FSi-SiO2@MMT, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were observed to exhibit a diffraction peak at 2θ = 5.68° and 4.24° in their XRD patterns, which were attributed to the (001) reflection of MMT and OMMT [44,50,51]. However, the d(001) diffraction peaks of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were found to disappear owing to the exfoliation of the MMT and OMMT multilayer structure after the insertion of LLDPE chains. In addition, after the dissociation of MMT and OMMT into single inorganic sheet, a uniform distribution was achieved in the LLDPE matrix, and a peel-off PE-based dust-proof composite film was finally prepared. These above results were consistent with the result of cross-sectional SEM images of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. Thermal stability of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was investigated by using thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA). As shown in Fig. 3c, the PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibited higher stability compared to PE. The initial decomposition temperatures of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were 364.08 °C, 393.12 °C, and 422.16 °C, respectively, indicating about 29.04 °C and 58.08 °C increment and improvement of thermal stability of PE. And the increment and improvement of thermal stability was attributed to the barrier effect of the MMT and OMMT. Compared with PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT possess more superior thermal stability. UV-light was divided into UV-A (400–320 nm), UV-B (320–280 nm), and UV-C (280–10 nm) radiation [52]. The enhancement of UV-B radiation could lead to the damage of the plant biofilm system, affect the photosynthesis intensity of the plant, and result in poor plant development and quality damage. Therefore, the transmittance of PE and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT in UV area was determined. As shown in Fig. 3d, the transmittance of PE in UV-A (400–320 nm), UV-B (320–280 nm), and UV-C (280–200 nm) bands was 36.33 %, 28.48 %, and 23.53 %, respectively. Meanwhile, the transmittance of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was 35.49 %, 26.41 %, and 18.71 %, respectively, which was lower than PE, suggesting PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT could effectively block harmful light in UV-B range and enhance photosynthesis of crops. The incorporated CDs render the composite films with photoluminescence (PL) properties. As displayed in Fig. 3e, two excitation peaks were observed when the emission wavelength was 426 nm. The PL emission spectra of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT under excitations from 335 to 375 nm were acquired and displayed in Fig. 3f, which indicated the PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibited a wavelength-dependent PL emission. Additionally, PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT could emit green and red lights after excited, which overlap the absorption wavelength of chloroplasts, suggesting PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT could enhance the photosynthesis efficiency of crops, then improving the growth and development of crops. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the PL intensity was decreased, signifying the photosynthetic activity of crops would be reduced. However, solar radiation possesses broad range, thus the PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT still increase light absorption in plants [53]. The obtained results indicated PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT possess the potential as light conversion composite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface wettability and abrasion resistance PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT
ce:section.ce:para: The WCA and SA of FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were measured using automatic contact angle meter. As shown in Fig. S6a, the WCA and SA of FSi-SiO2@MMT were 155.62 ± 1° and 4.8 ± 0.2°, respectively. Fig. S6b displayed the WCA and SA of FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were 169.22 ± 2° and 2.3 ± 0.2°, respectively. These above results indicated that FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were ideal superhydrophobic materials with potential as dust-proof additives for APF. Furthermore, the wettability of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was also tested by automatic contact angle meter. Fig. 4a shown the WCA of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was 74.1°, 101.25°, and 108.9°, respectively, suggesting the hydrophobicity was enhanced after the insertion of FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. Additionally, the SFE was also calculated based on the Owens-Wendt-Rabel-Kaelble (OWRK) method. It is noted that the SFE of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT (19.65 mN/m) was the lowest which compared with PE (39.75 mN/m) and PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT (24.35 mN/m). These above results indicated PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibit high hydrophobicity. For an ideal APF, the transmittance should higher than 87 % and the haze should lower than 30 % according to GB/T 4455-2019. Therefore, the transmittance and haze of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were evaluated through WGW photoelectric haze meter. Fig. 4b revealed that with the addition of FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT, the transmittance and haze of composite films were reduced, but they were all within the ideal range and the photosynthesis of crops was not affected. Furthermore, the mechanical property of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was also evaluated due to it is crucial for agricultural application. As shown in Table S1, the tensile strength and elongation at break of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were better than PE and PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT. The horizontal and portrait tensile strength improved from 25.08 and 27.41 MPa to 33.14 and 42.21 MPa, respectively, the horizontal and portrait elongation at break improved from 273.53 % and 380.23–369.43 % and 480.73 %, respectively. This revealed a simultaneous enhancement of toughness and stiffness for PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT, mainly attributed to the following points: (1) the grafting of a large amount of silane coupling agent on the surface of OMMT, which acted as a “bridge” between inorganic substances and polymers and then improved their interfacial adhesion; (2) the lamellar structure of the composite films also acted as a “crosslinking point” in the LLDPE matrix; (3) FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT had a certain orientation during the dispersion process of LLDPE matrix. Fig. 4c showed the WCA of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT after multiple folds. The initial WCA of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was 101.25° and 108.9°, respectively, and after 300 cycles of folds, the WCA of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was decreased to 100.98° and 107.5°, indicating the effect of repeated half-fold test on the composite micro-nano structure of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was negligible. Fig. 4d displayed the WCA of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT after sandpaper wear. The WCA of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was decreased 5.6° and 4.7° after moving 50 cm on the surface of the sandpaper. Furthermore, after moving 100 cm on the surface of the sandpaper, the WCA of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was decreased 15.05° and 8.58°, demonstrating sandpaper had adverse effects on the multi-scale micro-nano structure on the surface of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. Compared with PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibited better wear resistance owing to the more superior dispersion of FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT in LLDPE matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-fouling and dust-proof performance of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT
ce:section.ce:para: The self-cleaning performance of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was evaluated by using BTM solution ( Fig. 5a-b, Table S2, and Video 1–3). Fig. 5b and Video 1–3 displayed the detailed process of self-cleaning of composite films, in which the composite films without leaving traces of BTM solution were considered to possess excellent self-cleaning performance. The red circle in the Fig. 5b refers to the BTM solution remaining on the surface of composite films, and there were almost no BTM solution on the surface of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. Furthermore, the percentage of retention of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was calculated to be 7.39 %, 0.22 %, and 0.16 % according to Table S2, respectively. These above results indicated PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibited the best self-cleaning performance. Additionally, the dust-proof performance of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was also evaluated under simulated natural wind conditions. As displayed in Fig. 5c, the dust on the surface of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT gradually decreased with increasing exposure time. Interestingly, the dust removing efficiency of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was reached to 99.55 % after exposure 20 min, and the PE and PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT groups only up to 83.41 % and 93.23 %, respectively. After 40 min of exposure, the dust removing efficiency of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was calculated to be 89.25 %, 99.14 %, and 99.58 %, respectively. Comprehensive experimental results analysis, PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibited the best dust-proof performance, which could be attributed to their low SFE, suggesting PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT possess great potential in agriculture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-algae and anti-bacterial adhesion performance of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT
ce:section.ce:para: Besides dust, the light transmittance of APF was also affected by algae and bacteria. The Chlorella inhibition performance of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was shown in Fig. 6a. It is obvious observed that the attachments of Chlorella increased with the increase of time. At the end of 7 D assay period, a large amount of Chlorella adhesion could be observed on the surface of PE and a dense Chlorella membrane was formed. However, on the surface of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT, only a small amount of Chlorella was adhered, suggesting the growth of Chlorella was inhibited. Moreover, the anti-algae performance of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was better than PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT which could be attributed the low SFE and multiscale micro-nano structures. The multi-scale micro-nano structure of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT could capture a lot of air and fill it in the gaps between the structures. At this time, the interfacial area between the gas and liquid was significantly increased, and the Chlorella was hardly to penetrate. The anti-bacterial adhesion of PE, PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was investigated by plate counting method using Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). The bacterial colonies of bacteria on the surface of composite films were shown in Fig. 6b. Large number of colonies were observed when the bacterial suspension co-cultured with PE was diluted 105 times. Meanwhile, almost no colonies could be observed in the group of PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT and PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. Moreover, even the bacterial suspension was diluted 109 times, the presence of colonies could still be observed in PE group. Compared with PE/FSi-SiO2@MMT, PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT possess more excellent anti-bacterial adhesion property. Generally speaking, bacterial adhesion mainly involved three processes, including adsorption of bacteria on the surface of the material, secretion of extracellular polysaccharide matrix, and biofilm formation [54]. The excellent anti-bacterial adhesion property of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT was mainly attributed to multi-scale micro-nano structure, which makes air cushion stably entrapped at liquid/solid interfaces, avoiding direct contact of bacteria with PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT [55–57]. Additionally, bacterial adhesion would be further decreased due to the low SFE of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT [58]. These results suggested that PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibited a fascinating application prospect for APF.

In this study, a self-cleaning dust-proof APF with “lotus-effect” has been prepared by blending with FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT and LLDPE. Among them, FSi-SiO2 and CDs imparted multi-scale micro-nano structure to PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT. Moreover, the presence of CDs also gave PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT excellent light conversion performance, which was more conducive to the growth of crops. PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT exhibited superior hydrophobic and mechanical properties, as well as thermal stability. The WCA of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT could reach to 108.9°, which is expected to apply in self-cleaning field. Compared to PE, the horizontal and portrait tensile strength of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT increased by 8.06 and 14.8 MPa, respectively. PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT also exhibited excellent anti-fouling and dust-proof performance, with an anti-fouling rate and dust removal efficiency of 99.84 % and 99.58 %, respectively. In addition, PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT also possess good anti-algae and anti-bacterial adhesion properties, which is beneficial to improve the light transmittance of APF, thereby increasing the yield of crops. Also, the preparation raw materials and method of PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT are inexpensive and facile. Hence, it is expected that PE/FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT to be a feasible strategy for a wide range of agricultural applications.

Ninglin Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Li Jun: Funding acquisition. Jian Shen: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Xiaohong Chu: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Pan Zhang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Shi Shaoze: Formal analysis, Investigation. Yihan Liu: Formal analysis. Wenli Feng: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by Jiangsu Science and Technology Plan (BE 2019391), Key agricultural R & D program of Jiangsu Province (Modern Agriculture) (BE 2020347), Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, Shandong Natural Science Foundation (ZR2021LSW001, ZR2021MC017, ZR2020KH019, ZR201807060862).
